attractions.tips: Visit at sunset for spectacular views of the Oakland skyline reflected in the lake. The lake's walking path is popular for jogging and walking — mornings and evenings are less crowded. Birdwatchers should visit during rainy season (November–March) for the best variety. Free boat rentals are available at the Lakeside Park boathouse on weekends. The area is well-connected by BART (Lake Merritt station nearby).
attractions.nearby: Oakland Museum of California, Jack London Square, Fox Theater, Oakland Chinatown, Art Murmur gallery district, Piedmont Avenue shopping, Dimond District, Grand Lake Theatre, Oakland Zoo, Redwood Regional Park

attractions.name: Lick Observatory
attractions.description: Lick Observatory is an astronomical observatory owned and operated by the University of California, located on the summit of Mount Hamilton in the Diablo Range, approximately 20 miles east of San Jose. Built between 1876 and 1887 from a bequest by James Lick, it is the world's first permanently occupied mountain-top observatory. The observatory houses several research telescopes including the 36-inch Great Refractor, the second-largest refractor telescope in the world.
attractions.hours: Outdoor Areas & Parking: Daily 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM. Shane Visitor's Gallery: Daily 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM. Visitor Center & Gift Shop: Saturday & Sunday 12:00 PM – 5:00 PM (closed Mon–Fri). Free telescope talks on Saturdays & Sundays at 12:30, 1:30, 2:30, 3:30, and 4:30 PM.
attractions.days_open: Thursday through Sunday (Visitor Center & Gift Shop); Daily for outdoor areas and visitor's gallery
attractions.admission: Free for all visitors. No admission charge. Summer evening tours (June–September) require ticket purchase; SVP Summer Series price is approximately $5.
highlights: World's first permanently occupied mountain-top observatory
Home to the 36-inch Great Refractor, the second-largest refractor telescope in the world
Multiple research telescopes including the 3-meter Shane Telescope, Nickel Telescope, and Automated Planet Finder
Free guided tours of the historic dome and refractor telescope
Panoramic views of the Bay Area from 4,209 feet elevation
Vending machines, water fountain, and restrooms available on-site
attractions.tips: The drive to the summit takes about 1 hour from San Jose and 2 hours from San Francisco — the road is long, winding, and narrow, so allow plenty of time and drive carefully. Visit on clear days for the best views of the Bay Area and Santa Clara Valley. No reservations needed for daytime visits, but summer evening programs require advance ticket purchase. The observatory is currently closed for some visitor services — check the official website for the latest reopening updates. Bring layers as it can be chilly at 4,200 feet elevation.
attractions.nearby: Mount Hamilton State Park, Alum Rock Park, San Jose (downtown), Henry W. Coe State Park, Pacheco State Park

attractions.name: Lindsay Wildlife Experience
attractions.description: Lindsay Wildlife Experience, formerly Lindsay Wildlife Museum, is a family museum and wildlife rehabilitation center in Walnut Creek, California. It is the first wildlife hospital established in the United States, founded in 1955, treating over 5,000 injured, sick, or orphaned native wild animals annually. The museum features live California wildlife exhibits, natural history specimens, and educational programs that connect people with nature to inspire respect and responsibility.
attractions.hours: 10 a.m. to 5 p.m.
attractions.admission: Adults $17, Seniors 65+ $15, Children 2-17/Students $15, Qualifying EBT/Museums for All $3 per person (up to 4), Free for members and children under 2 [lindsaywildlife.org/hours-prices-directions](https://lindsaywildlife.org/hours-prices-directions/)
highlights: First U.S. wildlife hospital, treating 5,000+ native animals yearly
Live non-releasable California wildlife exhibits including raptors and reptiles
Behind-the-scenes view of wildlife treatment rooms
Interactive exhibits like Raptor Gallery with virtual Mt. Diablo flight and Hive Alive!
Daily animal ambassador programs and outdoor experiences
Educational programs, summer camps, and wildlife rehab focus
attractions.tips: Book timed programs in advance; check for special closures/holidays; use EBT for Museums for All discount; pack picnic for nearby Larkey Park; keep cats indoors to protect wildlife.
attractions.nearby: Larkey Park (playground, picnic areas), Lesher Center for the Arts, Mount Diablo State Park [lindsaywildlife.org](https://lindsaywildlife.org/), [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lindsay_Wildlife_Experience)

attractions.name: Livermore Valley Wine Country
attractions.description: Livermore Valley Wine Country is one of California's oldest wine regions, with a winemaking history dating back to the 1840s. Located just east of San Francisco Bay in Alameda County, the region is home to over 50 wineries and is known for its welcoming, family-owned tasting rooms where visitors can often meet the winemaker behind the bar. The valley is recognized for its distinctive gravelly soils and Mediterranean climate, producing award-winning wines across a wide variety of varietals.
attractions.hours: No fixed hours – the Livermore Valley Wine Country is a region comprising 50+ individual wineries, each with their own tasting room hours. Individual wineries typically operate 7 days a week with varying hours (commonly 11:00 AM – 5:00 PM or later).
attractions.days_open: 7 days a week (varies by individual winery)
attractions.admission: No admission fee to visit the region. Individual wineries typically charge $20–$50 per person for tasting flights, depending on the winery and wine selection. Some wineries waive tasting fees with bottle purchase. The 2026 Wine Passport is available for purchase as a multi-winery tasting package.
highlights: Over 50 wineries in a compact, easily accessible region just minutes from San Francisco and San Jose
California's oldest wine region with winemaking history dating back to the 1840s
Family-owned wineries where visitors can often meet the winemaker personally
Award-winning wines across diverse varietals, known for exceptional quality-to-value
Annual events including Wine Wednesday, Wine Month (May), and the Uncorked! celebration
Scenic vineyard landscapes with rolling hills and a Mediterranean climate
attractions.tips: Book tasting appointments in advance, especially on weekends, as popular wineries fill up quickly. Consider purchasing the 2026 Wine Passport for a multi-winery tasting experience. Use the official itinerary planner on lvwine.org to map your route between wineries. Designate a driver or book a wine tour service for safety. Visit on weekdays for a more intimate experience with smaller crowds. Check each winery's website for current hours and tasting fees before visiting.
attractions.nearby: Downtown Livermore (historic shopping and dining district), Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory Discovery Center, Livermore Area Recreation & Park District parks and trails, San Francisco Premium Outlets, Alameda County Fairgrounds, Livermore Rodeo, Livermore Valley Performing Arts Center, East Bay Regional Parks

attractions.name: Mission Peak Regional Preserve
attractions.description: Mission Peak Regional Preserve is a 3,023-acre regional park in the Diablo Range east of Fremont, California, operated by the East Bay Regional Park District. The preserve features a dramatic 2,517-foot summit that offers panoramic views of the South Bay, East Bay, and on clear days, the Pacific Ocean. Two main trailheads provide access — the Ohlone College entrance (more gradual ascent) and the Stanford Avenue entrance (steeper climb). The preserve is one of the most popular hiking destinations in the Bay Area.
attractions.hours: Ohlone College Entrance/Parking Lot: Daily, year-round, 6 a.m. - 10 p.m. | Stanford Avenue Entrance/Parking Lot: Dec 23 - Mar 31: 6:30 a.m. - 8 p.m.; Apr 1 - Dec 22: 6:30 a.m. - 9 p.m.
attractions.admission: Free admission. Parking at Ohlone College entrance requires a valid parking permit displayed 6 a.m. - 11 p.m. every day (excludes college holidays and semester breaks). Stanford Avenue parking is limited to 43 spaces with a residential parking permit program.
highlights: 2,517-foot summit with panoramic views of the South Bay, East Bay, and Pacific Ocean
3,023 acres of rolling hills, oak woodlands, and grasslands
Two main trailheads: Ohlone College (gradual 5.6-mile round trip) and Stanford Avenue (steep 6-mile round trip)
Wildlife viewing including free-roaming cattle, coyotes, and deer
Popular training ground for hikers and runners of all levels
Peak Trail and Panorama Trail leading to the summit
attractions.tips: Start early (by 7 a.m.) to avoid crowds and heat. The trail has very little shade — bring plenty of water, sun protection, and wear layers. Parking fills up extremely fast on weekends; consider rideshare or parking in nearby neighborhoods. The Ohlone College entrance offers a more gradual, longer ascent with ample parking (900+ spaces), while Stanford Avenue is steeper with limited parking (43 spaces). Cell reception is available for most of the hike. Allow 3-4 hours for the round trip. The park closes at 10 p.m. from Ohlone and 8-9 p.m. from Stanford — plan accordingly. Consider visiting during winter/spring for cooler temperatures and wildflowers.
attractions.nearby: Coyote Hills Regional Park, Ardenwood Historic Farm, Alameda Creek Trail, Quarry Lakes Regional Recreation Area, Sunol Regional Wilderness, Fremont Central Park, Lake Elizabeth

attractions.name: Mount Diablo State Park
attractions.description: Mount Diablo State Park is a California state park encompassing approximately 20,000 acres (8,000 hectares) centered around Mount Diablo, an isolated upthrust peak of 3,849 feet (1,173 meters) in Contra Costa County. The park was the first public open space established on or near the peak and offers panoramic views of the San Francisco Bay Area, with the Sierra Nevada visible on clear days. The mountain is accessible by foot, bicycle, or motor vehicle via North Gate Road or South Gate Road, and features diverse vegetation, wildlife, and recreational opportunities.
attractions.address: North Gate Road, 2000 Mitchell Canyon Road
attractions.hours: Daily: 5:00 a.m. to Sunset, year round (park gates); Summit Visitor Center hours vary seasonally
attractions.admission: Day Use Fee: $10 per vehicle (standard day use fee for California State Parks). Additional fees may apply for special events or facilities.
highlights: Panoramic 360-degree summit views visible from the San Francisco Bay Area to the Sierra Nevada on clear days
Summit Visitor Center with museum exhibits and telescope for viewing
Over 115 miles of hiking, biking, and equestrian trails
Diverse ecosystems ranging from grasslands to oak woodlands and chaparral
Wildlife viewing including coyotes, bobcats, deer, and numerous bird species
Historical significance with Native American cultural sites and early California history
attractions.tips: Visit on clear days after a winter storm for the best visibility — the Sierra Nevada can be seen from the summit. Arrive early on weekends as parking fills quickly. Bring water and sun protection as the summit area has limited shade. Check for fire restrictions during dry summer months as they may affect access. The summit road can be foggy in the morning; afternoon visits often offer clearer views.
attractions.nearby: Black Diamond Mines Regional Preserve, Shell Ridge Open Space, Morgan Territory Regional Preserve, Round Valley Regional Preserve, Las Trampas Regional Wilderness

attractions.name: NASA Ames Visitor Center
attractions.description: The NASA Ames Visitor Center is a hands-on exhibition located within Chabot Space & Science Center in Oakland, California. It brings to life the thrilling, challenging, and inspiring process of scientific discovery by showcasing the real stories and people at NASA's Ames Research Center. Visitors step into the role of a NASA scientist through interactive challenges, exploring more than 30+ objects that showcase Ames' past and future while getting to know real NASA scientists.
attractions.hours: Friday - Sunday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM (through May 31st)
attractions.days_open: Friday, Saturday, Sunday (closed Monday through Thursday)
attractions.admission: Chabot Members: Free; Adults (12+): $24; Children (2-12): $17; Seniors (65+): $17
highlights: Hands-on exhibition featuring more than 30+ NASA artifacts and objects
Interactive challenges where visitors step into the role of a NASA scientist
Showcases the real stories and people at NASA's Ames Research Center
Located in Fremont Bank Studio 1 at Chabot Space & Science Center
Explores Ames' past missions and future space exploration projects
attractions.tips: The NASA Ames Visitor Center is now housed within Chabot Space & Science Center, which is open only Friday-Sunday. Plan your visit accordingly. Admission to the NASA exhibit is included with general Chabot admission. The original NASA Ames Visitor Center location at Moffett Field in Mountain View has been permanently closed. Allow 1-2 hours to explore the exhibit. Check Chabot's website for current hours as they may change seasonally.
attractions.nearby: Chabot Observatory and Planetarium, Redwood Regional Park, Oakland Zoo, Lake Chabot Regional Park, Joaquin Miller Park

attractions.name: Niles Canyon Railway
attractions.description: Niles Canyon Railway is a living history railroad museum operated by the Pacific Locomotive Association, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization. It offers historic steam and diesel train excursions through scenic Niles Canyon between Sunol and Niles (Fremont), California. The railway preserves the atmosphere of small-town railroading in America before the 1960s, operating on a historic line that was part of the original transcontinental railroad route.
attractions.address: 6 Kilkare Road (Sunol Station) / 37029 Mission Blvd (Niles Station)
attractions.hours: Regular train rides: Second and third weekends (Saturday and Sunday) of each month, March through October, with departures at 10:30 AM and 1:00 PM. Special events and Train of Lights operate on select dates November through December. Closed Monday through Friday.
attractions.days_open: Saturday and Sunday (second and third weekends of each month, March through October), plus select dates for special events and holiday season
attractions.admission: Diesel train rides: Adults $25, Seniors (62+) and Children (3-12) $15. Steam train rides: Adults $30, Seniors (62+) and Children (3-12) $20. Children 2 and under free. Train of Lights: General Seating $40, First Class Dome $70, First Class Parlor $70, Private Caboose $850. Speeder Rides: $35 per person.
highlights: Historic steam and diesel locomotive-powered train excursions through scenic Niles Canyon
Part of the original transcontinental railroad route with 120+ years of history
Operating railroad museum with authentic vintage passenger cars including dome cars and cabooses
Annual Train of Lights holiday event with decorated trains running at dusk and after dark
Speeder rides offering a unique railcar experience through the canyon
Educational programs for schools, Boy Scout Railroad Merit Badge program, and community events
attractions.tips: Arrive 30-45 minutes before departure time to pick up tickets and board. Purchase tickets online in advance as rides frequently sell out. Dress in layers as canyon weather can be cool and windy, especially for open-air cars. Check which station (Sunol or Niles) your departure is from before traveling. For Train of Lights, bring a small flashlight for moving between your car and the train at night. Children under 3 are free but need a ticket if they occupy a seat.
attractions.nearby: Niles Historic District (downtown Niles with antique shops and restaurants), Niles Essanay Silent Film Museum, Quarry Lakes Regional Recreation Area, Sunol Regional Wilderness, Ardenwood Historic Farm, Pleasanton (Tri-Valley area with shopping and dining)

attractions.name: Oakland Chinatown
attractions.description: Oakland Chinatown is one of the oldest Chinatowns in the United States, dating back to the 1850s. It is a vibrant "working Chinatown" where residents live, work, shop, and dine rather than a tourist-focused district. The neighborhood is home to a diverse pan-Asian community including Chinese, Vietnamese, Korean, Filipino, Japanese, Cambodian, Laotian, Mien, and Thai residents, with over 45 restaurants and 9 tea shops. Bounded by Broadway to the west, Interstate 880 to the south, Fallon Street to the east, and 12th Street to the north, it represents a living piece of Asian American history in the East Bay.
attractions.address: Centered at 8th Street and Webster Street, Oakland, CA 94607
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (public neighborhood). Individual businesses vary: most shops and restaurants operate during daytime hours, with some late-night eateries open until early morning.
attractions.days_open: Daily (24/7 as a public neighborhood; individual business hours vary)
attractions.admission: Free – no tickets required to visit Oakland Chinatown. It is a public neighborhood. Special events like the Chinatown Street Festival may have associated costs for specific activities.
attractions.phone: Not available – Oakland Chinatown is a public neighborhood with no central phone number. The Oakland Chinatown Chamber of Commerce is at https://www.oaklandchinatownchamber.org
highlights: Pacific Renaissance Plaza – a mixed-use complex with restaurants, shops, banks, the Asian Branch Library, and Oakland Asian Cultural Center
Oakland Asian Cultural Center – promoting pan-Asian arts, culture, workshops, dance, martial arts, and exhibitions
Oakland Fortune Cookie Factory – one of the oldest fortune cookie makers in the Bay Area, operating since 1957
Oakland Chinatown Pedestrian Scramble – the first pedestrian scramble design in the Bay Area, implemented in 2002
Lincoln Square Park – community park with basketball courts and a distinctive Chinese junk ship play structure
Over 45 restaurants and 9 tea shops featuring authentic Asian cuisine from Cantonese to Vietnamese to Korean
attractions.tips: Visit during 10am-3pm to experience the authentic local shopping and dining atmosphere. Parking can be challenging; consider taking BART to Lake Merritt Station or 12th Street/Oakland City Center Station. Visit late at night for authentic late-night dining at spots like New Gold Medal (open until 3am) and Shooting Star Cafe. Don't miss the pedestrian scramble at the intersection of 8th and Webster Streets. The best time to visit is during annual events like the Lunar New Year Bazaar (January/February) or the Chinatown Street Festival (August). Be respectful as this is a working neighborhood, not a tourist attraction.
attractions.nearby: Lake Merritt, Jack London Square, Oakland Asian Cultural Center, Oakland Public Library Asian Branch, Madison Square Park, Chinatown BART Station (12th Street/Oakland City Center), Lake Merritt BART Station, Oakland Museum of California

attractions.name: Oakland Museum of California (OMCA)
attractions.description: The Oakland Museum of California (OMCA) is an interdisciplinary museum dedicated to the art, history, and natural science of California, located at 1000 Oak Street in Oakland. The museum contains more than 1.8 million objects telling the extraordinary story of California. Founded in 1969 as a merger of three smaller museums, OMCA is known as a "people's museum" dedicated to representing the diverse communities of Oakland and California.
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Sunday: 11 AM–5 PM; Open Late Fridays: 11 AM–9 PM; Monday–Tuesday: Closed
attractions.admission: General Admission (Galleries of California Art, History, & Natural Sciences): Adult (18-64) $19, Senior (65+) $16, Youth (13-17)/Students/Educators with Valid ID $12, Child (12 & Under) FREE*, Museums for All EBT Card Holders $1. Special Exhibitions: +$6. Members: FREE (includes special exhibitions). *Free admission for children provided by The Bernard Osher Foundation.
highlights: Gallery of California Art — featuring over 70,000 examples of California art and design from the mid-1800s to present, including works by Dorothea Lange, Richard Diebenkorn, and Mel Ramos
Gallery of California History — more than 1.8 million items representing California's history and cultures from pre-European era to the 21st century
Gallery of California Natural Sciences — showcasing California's natural environment, biodiversity, and ecosystems
OMCA Garden — free rooftop garden and terrace with views of Lake Merritt and downtown Oakland
Friday Nights at OMCA with Off the Grid — Oakland's favorite weekly block party featuring food trucks, live music, and art
Tony Labat's 'Big Peace IV' — iconic yellow peace sign sculpture on the museum's rooftop
attractions.tips: Visit on the first Sunday of every month for free general admission (First Sundays at OMCA). The museum is closed Monday and Tuesday. The Garden is free to enjoy during regular museum hours — you don't need a ticket. Free Wi-Fi and phone charging stations are available. Free lockers are provided on all three levels. The Town Fare Cafe offers Sunday brunch (11 AM–4 PM) but doesn't take reservations, so arrive early for Soul Sunday Buffet on the last Sunday of each month. OMCA is one block from the Lake Merritt BART station.
attractions.nearby: Lake Merritt, Old Oakland (historic district), Jack London Square, Oakland Chinatown, Grand Lake Theatre, Fox Theater, Oakland Asian Cultural Center, Chabot Space & Science Center

attractions.name: Oakland Zoo
attractions.description: Oakland Zoo is home to more than 850 native and exotic animals across 100 acres in the hills of Knowland Park. Founded in 1922, it is one of the four largest zoos in California and attracts nearly 900,000 guests annually. The zoo is managed by the Conservation Society of California, a nonprofit organization dedicated to wildlife conservation locally and globally.
attractions.hours: Monday–Friday: Admission 9:30am–2:00pm, Zoo closes 4:00pm. Gondola opens 9:30am, last ride up 2:30pm, last ride down 3:30pm. Rides 10:30am–3:30pm. Petting Yard 10:00am–3:00pm. Habitarium 11:00am–3:00pm. Gift Shop 9:30am–3:30pm. Weekends and Holidays: Admission 9:30am–3:00pm, Zoo closes 4:00pm. Gondola opens 9:30am, last ride up 3:30pm, last ride down 4:30pm. Rides 10:30am–4:30pm. Petting Yard 10:00am–3:00pm. Habitarium 11:00am–3:00pm. Skyride 10:30am–3:00pm. Gift Shop 9:30am–4:30pm.
attractions.days_open: Daily (closed select holidays and for private events/maintenance)
attractions.admission: Plan-Ahead Pricing (prices vary daily). Child tickets (age 2-14) are 15% off Adult price. Senior tickets are 10% off Adult price. Children under 2 are free. Parking: $13 online in advance, $16 at entry. Unlimited Rides Wristbands: $20 General Admission, $15 Members. Discounted ticket books: $45 for 16 tickets. Discounts available for Oakland residents (Measure Y), teachers, active-duty military/veterans, and AZA reciprocal members (50% off). Bay to Zoo program: $5 adult, $3 senior/child for qualifying low-income residents.
highlights: California Trail with gondola ride offering panoramic Bay Area views
Over 850 native and exotic animals including elephants, tigers, lions, and gorillas
Children's Zoo with petting yard and interactive experiences
Wild Australia exhibit featuring kangaroos, emus, and koalas
Tiger Trek roller coaster and conservation carousel rides
Sky Ride cable car attraction (weekends and holidays only)
attractions.tips: Advanced reservations are required for general admission. Book tickets online in advance for lower prices via the Plan-Ahead Pricing system. Purchase parking online ($13 vs $16 at entry) to save money. Arrive early for the best animal viewing experience. Consider a membership if planning multiple visits. The gondola ride is included with admission and offers spectacular views. Take AC Transit Line 46L on weekdays to avoid parking hassles.
attractions.nearby: Redwood Regional Park, Joaquin Miller Park, Dimond District, Lake Merritt, Oakland Museum of California, Fruitvale District

attractions.name: Point Richmond Historic District
attractions.description: The Point Richmond Historic District is the downtown area of Point Richmond in Richmond, California, listed on the National Register of Historic Places since November 5, 1979. Developed in the late 1890s when the San Francisco and San Joaquin Valley Railway occupied 57 acres, it served as Richmond's original downtown before the city expanded. Today it features Victorian-era architecture, historic buildings, and small mom-and-pop shops housed in century-old structures.
attractions.address: Point Richmond Historic District, Richmond, CA 94801
attractions.hours: Open 24/7 (outdoor public historic district with no restricted hours)
attractions.admission: Free - No admission fee (public outdoor historic district)
attractions.phone: Not available (no official phone number for the district itself)
highlights: Listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1979
Victorian-era architecture and historic buildings from the 1890s-1900s
Point Richmond History Museum showcasing the area's railroad and oil refinery heritage
Historic Hotel Mac (built 1911) listed in the National Register of Historic Buildings
Charming mom-and-pop shops in century-old buildings
Walking tour maps available from Richmond Convention and Visitors Bureau
attractions.tips: Pick up a walking tour map from the Richmond Convention and Visitors Bureau to explore the historic zone. The district is easily accessible from Interstate 580, Richmond Parkway, Macdonald Avenue, and Cutting Boulevard. AC Transit bus route 72M connects to Richmond BART station. Visit nearby attractions like the Point Richmond History Museum, West Side Library, and Alvarado Park. Parking can be limited on busy days, so arrive early.
attractions.nearby: Richmond Municipal Natatorium, Point Richmond History Museum, West Side Library, Alvarado Park/Wildcat Canyon Regional Park, Richmond-San Rafael Bridge, Chevron Richmond Refinery (viewing area), Point San Pablo Peninsula, Rosie the Riveter/WWII Home Front National Historical Park

attractions.name: Rosie the Riveter WWII Home Front National Historical Park
attractions.description: Rosie the Riveter WWII Home Front National Historical Park celebrates and honors the contributions and sacrifices of American civilians on the World War II home front. Located in Richmond, California, it preserves the historic sites where diverse communities lived, worked, and interacted during the war years. The park includes museums, historic shipyards, housing sites, and other landmarks that tell the story of the home front experience.
attractions.hours: 10:00 AM–5:00 PM daily
attractions.days_open: Open daily (closed on Thanksgiving Day, Christmas Day, and New Year's Day)
attractions.admission: Free — no entrance fee or pass required year-round
highlights: Visitor Education Center with exhibits on WWII home front history
Historic Ford Assembly Plant building
SS Red Oak Victory ship museum
Richmond Shipyards where Liberty Ships were built
Kaiser Permanente Field Hospital historic site
Multiple historic housing sites from the wartime era
attractions.tips: Start your visit at the Visitor Education Center at 1414 Harbour Way South, Suite 3000. The park is fee-free. Allow 2-3 hours to explore the visitor center exhibits. Plan ahead for tours of specific sites like the SS Red Oak Victory, which may have limited hours. Check the NPS website or call ahead for current conditions and closures.
attractions.nearby: Point Richmond Historic District, Richmond Marina, Craneway Pavilion, Pt. Isabel Regional Shoreline, Richmond Art Center, Berkeley Marina

attractions.name: Shadelands Ranch Museum
attractions.description: Shadelands Ranch is a historic house museum in Walnut Creek, California, established in 1856 by Hiram Penniman as one of the first and largest farms in California's Ygnacio Valley. The Colonial Revival-style ranch house is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and is managed by the Walnut Creek Historical Society. The museum offers docent-led tours of the house and grounds, showcasing over 165 years of local history.
attractions.address: 2660 Ygnacio Valley Road, Walnut Creek, CA 94598
attractions.hours: Museum Tours (February 1 – October 28, 2026): Sundays & Wednesdays, 1:00 pm – 4:00 pm. Business (by appointment only): Monday 9:00 am – 3:00 pm, Tuesday 9:00 am – 3:00 pm, Thursday 9:00 am – 3:00 pm. Friday & Saturday: Closed. Closed on all major public holidays.
attractions.days_open: Sundays and Wednesdays (public tours); Monday, Tuesday, Thursday (by appointment only)
attractions.admission: Adults (17 and up): $5.00; Youth (16 and under): Free; WCHS Members: Free. Group tours (8+ people) require reservations.
highlights: Colonial Revival-style historic ranch house on the National Register of Historic Places
Docent-led tours of the house and grounds during open season
Over 165 years of Walnut Creek history and heritage
2.7-acre property with historic gardens and grounds
Accessibility: grounds and first floor fully wheelchair accessible
Supplementary materials including videos and photos for visitors with special needs
attractions.tips: Plan your visit between February and October when the museum is open for public tours. Arrive during Sunday or Wednesday afternoon hours (1-4 pm) for docent-led tours. Closed on major public holidays including April 5 and July 5. Private tours can be scheduled on alternate days by calling ahead. The first floor and grounds are wheelchair accessible. Group tours (8+) require advance reservations.
attractions.nearby: Borges Ranch (historic cattle ranch), Walnut Creek downtown historic district, Mount Diablo State Park, Lesher Center for the Arts, Heather Farm Park

attractions.name: The Lawrence Hall of Science
attractions.description: The Lawrence Hall of Science is a public science center operated by the University of California, Berkeley, offering hands-on science exhibits, planetarium shows, and educational programs for all ages. Established in 1968 in honor of physicist Ernest Orlando Lawrence, the center is located in the hills above the UC Berkeley campus with stunning views of the San Francisco Bay. Visitors can explore permanent exhibits, a 3D theater, and outdoor science spaces including the Animal Discovery Zone.
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Sunday: 10:00 a.m.–5:00 p.m.; 'ammatka Cafe: 11:00 a.m.–3:00 p.m.; Animal Discovery Zone: 11:00 a.m.–4:00 p.m.; Closed Monday–Tuesday
attractions.admission: Adults & children ages 3+: $25; Children 2 & under: FREE; Members: FREE; UC Berkeley students & staff: FREE; Museums for All pass holders: FREE; Active-Duty Military: FREE; Planetarium: $5 + Admission; 3D Theater: $5 + Admission
highlights: Hands-on interactive science exhibits for all ages
Planetarium shows with $5 add-on
3D Theater with $5 add-on
Animal Discovery Zone featuring live animals
Stunning views of the San Francisco Bay from the terrace
Outdoor science exhibits and sculptures
attractions.tips: Reserve tickets in advance as they encourage it. Check weather conditions before visiting since many exhibits are outdoors. Allow 2-3 hours for a full visit. Free parking is available but fills up quickly on weekends — arrive early. The cafe is only open 11am-3pm, so plan meals accordingly.
attractions.nearby: UC Berkeley Botanical Garden, Tilden Regional Park, UC Berkeley campus, Grizzly Peak, Berkeley Rose Garden

attractions.name: University of California, Berkeley (UC Berkeley)
attractions.description: The University of California, Berkeley is a public land-grant research university founded in 1868, named after Anglo-Irish philosopher George Berkeley. It is the state's first land-grant university and the founding campus of the University of California system. With over 45,000 students and 32 libraries, UC Berkeley is classified as an R1 Doctoral University with very high research activity and has produced 63 Nobel laureates.
attractions.hours: Campus grounds are generally accessible from dawn to dusk. Academic buildings typically open around 7:00 AM and close between 5:00 PM and 10:00 PM depending on the building and day. The campus operates 24/7 during finals weeks.
attractions.days_open: Open daily (grounds accessible dawn to dusk; buildings vary by schedule)
attractions.admission: Free to walk the campus grounds. Some buildings may be restricted to students, faculty, and staff. Tourist/self-guided tours are free. Guided campus tours may require registration.
highlights: Sather Gate and Sather Tower (The Campanile) - iconic campus landmarks with panoramic views of the Bay Area
Hearst Greek Theatre - a stunning open-air amphitheater hosting concerts and events
Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory - pioneering research facility originally founded as part of the university
Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive (BAMPFA) - world-class art museum and film archive
Strawberry Creek - a scenic natural creek running through the center of campus
Memorial Glade and Doe Memorial Library - historic campus center and the largest library in the UC system
attractions.tips: Visit during a weekday to see the campus at its most active. The free guided campus tours depart from the Cal Visitor Center and provide the best introduction to the university's history and landmarks. Sather Tower (The Campanile) offers spectacular panoramic views but may require a separate ticket or have limited hours. Parking on campus is limited and expensive; consider taking BART to the Downtown Berkeley station and walking to campus. The Hearst Greek Theatre and Memorial Stadium are must-see architectural highlights.
attractions.nearby: Tilden Regional Park and Regional Parks Botanic Garden, UC Botanical Garden at Berkeley, Lawrence Hall of Science, Telegraph Avenue (shopping and dining district), Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive (BAMPFA), Berkeley Marina, and the University Art Museum.

attractions.name: Wente Vineyards
attractions.description: Wente Vineyards is the oldest continuously operating, family-owned winery in the United States, established in 1883 in Livermore, California. Located in the picturesque Livermore Valley just 30 miles from San Francisco Bay, the estate is registered as a California Historical Landmark (#957). The winery offers wine tastings, a restaurant, an 18-hole golf course, and hosts summer concerts. It is renowned for developing the iconic Wente Chardonnay Clone, which accounts for 75% of California's Chardonnay plantings.
attractions.hours: Monday–Wednesday: 12:00 PM – 5:30 PM (last tasting at 4:00 PM); Thursday–Sunday: 12:00 PM – 6:00 PM (last tasting at 4:30 PM)
attractions.admission: Tasting flights start at $40. "Two Gems of Livermore" experience (Wente + Murrieta's Well) available by appointment. Golf course green fees approximately $112. Wine club members receive complimentary tastings for up to four guests.
highlights: America's oldest continuously operating, family-owned winery (since 1883)
California Historical Landmark #957
Wente Chardonnay Clone - the most popular Chardonnay clone in California
Estate-grown Certified Sustainable wines from 2,000+ acres
The Course at Wente Vineyards - 18-hole Greg Norman-designed golf course
Summer concert venue hosting headliner performances
attractions.tips: Walk-ins are welcome but reservations are recommended for a better experience. The tasting room is family-friendly. Try the Nth Degree collection for premium small-lot wines. The patio offers beautiful shaded seating with vineyard views. Visit during summer for outdoor concerts. Golfers should note the course is not walkable - a cart is required.
attractions.nearby: Retzlaff Vineyards, Concannon Vineyard, McGrail Vineyards and Winery, Murrieta's Well Winery, Ravenswood Historic Site, Sycamore Grove Regional Park, The Steven Kent Winery, Darcie Kent Vineyards, Livermore Wine Trolley, Lawrence Livermore National Lab Discovery Center

attractions.name: Angel Island State Park
attractions.description: Angel Island State Park is the largest island in San Francisco Bay, administered by California State Parks. Known as the "Ellis Island of the West," it served as the U.S. Immigration Station from 1910 to 1940, processing approximately one million immigrants primarily from Asia. The island also features historic military installations, hiking trails, camping facilities, and stunning panoramic views of the Bay Area.
attractions.hours: Park is open daily from 8:00 AM to sunset. Ferry schedules vary by season: regular service in summer, reduced in spring/fall, and limited weekday service in winter. The U.S. Immigration Station Detention Barracks Museum is closed on Mondays and Tuesdays. Museums have limited hours based on season.
attractions.days_open: Daily (8:00 AM - sunset); Museum closed Mondays and Tuesdays
attractions.admission: Park admission is included with ferry tickets. Golden Gate Ferry (from SF): Adult $15.50 one-way with Clipper, $14 paper; Youth (5-18) $8.00; Senior (65+) $8.00; Child (4 & under) Free. Angel Island Tiburon Ferry: Adult $18.00 round-trip; Senior $16.00; Child (6-12) $15.00; Child (3-5) $6.00; Toddler (2 & under) Free; Bicycle $1.00. Private boat: Dock slip $15 (up to 8 people); Mooring $30 (day use, includes overnight). Immigration Station: Self-guided entry $5 adults, $3 youth (5-17), free under 5.
highlights: U.S. Immigration Station National Historic Landmark - 'Ellis Island of the West'
360-degree panoramic views of San Francisco, Sausalito, Belvedere Island, and the Bay
Extensive hiking and biking trail network including the perimeter road
Historic Fort McDowell military installations and WWII Mess Hall
Camping and overnight mooring facilities at Ayala Cove
Detention Barracks Museum with staged rooms reflecting historical detention conditions
attractions.tips: Check ferry schedules before visiting, especially in winter when service is limited. The ferries won't wait for you, so leave ample time to return. The Immigration Station Museum is closed Mondays and Tuesdays. Bring layers as it can be windy on the island. The tram tour is an excellent way to see the entire island and take in 360-degree views. Night travel after sunset is prohibited in some areas for safety. Advance ferry reservations are recommended, especially for the Tiburon ferry.
attractions.nearby: Alcatraz Island, Tiburon, Sausalito, Muir Woods National Monument, Mount Tamalpais State Park, Golden Gate Bridge, Richardson Bay

attractions.name: Bay Area Discovery Museum
attractions.description: Nestled at the foot of the Golden Gate Bridge in Sausalito, the Bay Area Discovery Museum is a children's museum where little bodies and big imaginations are free to run wild. It offers hands-on exhibits and outdoor discovery zones designed for children ages 0-10. The museum celebrates its 40th anniversary in 2026 and features a mix of indoor and outdoor interactive play spaces.
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Sunday: 10 AM–4 PM (Member Hour: 9–10 AM). Closed Mondays and Tuesdays. The museum closes at 3 PM on the second Thursday of each month.
attractions.admission: General Admission: Child (ages 1–17) $25, Adult (ages 18–64) $25, Senior (ages 65+) $23. Under 1 year FREE. Discount Admission: $5 for EBT/SNAP/WIC Cardholders (4 ticket limit per card). Members enjoy free admission and exclusive Member Hours 9–10 AM.
highlights: Outdoor Discovery Zones with hands-on exhibits for children
Views of the Golden Gate Bridge and San Francisco Bay
Woodshop and art-making spaces for creative play
40th Anniversary celebration in 2026
Free parking on-site
Cafe with food and snacks available
attractions.tips: Buy tickets online in advance. Members get exclusive early access from 9–10 AM. The museum closes at 3 PM on the second Thursday of each month. Check the Rainy Day Guarantee policy before visiting. Free parking is available. Apply your first visit ticket cost toward a membership.
attractions.nearby: Golden Gate Bridge, Sausalito waterfront, Cavallo Point Lodge, Fort Baker, Sausalito Ferry Terminal, Marin Headlands, Muir Woods National Monument

attractions.name: Benicia State Recreation Area
attractions.description: Benicia State Recreation Area is a 447-acre California state park protecting tidal marsh wetland, grassy hillsides, and rocky beaches along the narrowest portion of the Carquinez Strait. The park offers 2.5 miles of road and trails for cyclists, runners, walkers, equestrians, and roller skaters. It features the Southampton Bay Wetland Natural Preserve, which makes up 70% of the park and is a popular spot for fishing and wildlife viewing.
attractions.hours: Daily: 8:00 AM - Sunset
attractions.admission: Vehicle Day Use: $6.00
highlights: 447 acres of tidal marsh wetland, grassy hillsides, and rocky beaches along the Carquinez Strait
2.5 miles of paved and unpaved trails for cycling, running, walking, equestrian, and roller skating
Southampton Bay Wetland Natural Preserve covering 70% of the park with rich bird and wildlife habitat
Popular fishing spot at Dillon's Point marshland area
Scenic views of the narrowest portion of the Carquinez Strait where Sacramento and San Joaquin Rivers approach San Francisco Bay
Beaver lodge viewing area on Southampton Creek
attractions.tips: Wear layered clothing as weather can be changeable. The park is open from 8 AM to sunset daily. Parking fee of $6 applies for vehicle day use. Dogs are allowed on roads and trails. Collecting or destroying anything in the park, including mushrooms, is prohibited. The marshland area on Dillon's Point is a particularly favorite place for fishing.
attractions.nearby: Benicia Capitol State Historic Park, Downtown Benicia with shops and restaurants, Carquinez Strait Regional Shoreline, Glen Cove in Vallejo, Six Flags Discovery Kingdom, Vallejo Ferry Terminal

attractions.name: Bodega Bay
attractions.description: Bodega Bay is a picturesque fishing village on the Sonoma Coast in Northern California, known for its dramatic coastal scenery, fresh seafood, and as the filming location for Alfred Hitchcock's 1963 film The Birds. The bay is protected from the Pacific Ocean by Bodega Head, a peninsula offering whale watching and hiking opportunities. Located approximately 70 miles north of San Francisco, it serves as a gateway to the Sonoma Coast with charming shops, restaurants, and outdoor recreation.
attractions.hours: N/A - Bodega Bay is a public coastal town open 24/7. Individual businesses, attractions, and parks have their own hours.
attractions.days_open: Open year-round, 24 hours a day (as a public coastal town)
attractions.admission: Free - Bodega Bay is a public town with no admission fee. Individual attractions such as Doran Regional Park, nearby state parks, and commercial activities have their own separate fees.
highlights: Bodega Head - dramatic headland for whale watching and hiking with ocean vistas
Doran Regional Park - beach and salt marsh habitat for birdwatching and water sports
Filming location of Alfred Hitchcock's The Birds
Fresh seafood dining at spots like Tides Wharf and Fisherman's Cove
Part of the Sonoma Coast State Park system
Bodega Bay Seafood, Art, and Wine Festival held annually in late August
attractions.tips: Best whale watching is at Bodega Head in January/February and April/May. The town is about 70 miles (1.5 hours) north of San Francisco via Highway 1 or US-101 to Highway 116 then Highway 1. Many businesses close early on weekdays, so plan accordingly. The weather can be foggy and windy even in summer — bring layers. Parking can be limited at popular spots like Bodega Head during peak season.
attractions.nearby: Sonoma Coast State Park, Fort Ross State Historic Park, Jenner, Bodega (the village where The Birds was filmed), Dillon Beach, Tomales Bay, The Lodge at Bodega Bay, Sonoma County Wine Country, Russian River area

attractions.name: Mount Tamalpais State Park
attractions.description: Just north of San Francisco's Golden Gate Bridge, Mount Tamalpais State Park rises majestically from the heart of Marin County. Its deep canyons and sweeping hillsides are cloaked in dense oak woodlands and redwood groves, offering breathtaking vistas that include the Farallon Islands 25 miles out to sea, the Marin Coast, and the San Francisco Bay Area. The park features superlative hiking, picnicking, wildlife watching, camping, and the historic Mountain Theater that hosts outdoor plays and astronomy programs.
attractions.hours: Daily: 7:00 a.m. to Sunset, year round
attractions.admission: No main entrance fee. Parking fees apply at East Peak, Pantoll, and Bootjack parking areas. Day-use parking fee: $10. Annual Day-Use Pass also available.
highlights: Panoramic views of the Farallon Islands, Marin Coast, and San Francisco Bay Area from East Peak
Hiking trails including the accessible Verna Dunshee Trail and connections to Muir Woods
Historic Mountain Theater hosting outdoor plays and astronomy programs
Camping at Pantoll, Bootjack, Alice Eastwood Group Camp, and Steep Ravine Cabins
East Peak Visitor Center with volunteer-staffed information and merchandise
Gravity Car Barn showcasing the old Mount Tamalpais Scenic Railway history
attractions.tips: Arrive early to avoid heavy weekend and holiday traffic. Consider taking the West Marin Stage Coach. Dogs are only allowed on paved roads, Old Stage Fire Rd, Verna Dunshee Trail, and in parking areas — not on trails. Drones are prohibited. Be alert for poison oak, rattlesnakes, mountain lions, and ticks. Smoking, camping, and fires are never permitted in the upper mountain. Bring layers as weather can change quickly.
attractions.nearby: Muir Woods National Monument, Muir Beach, Stinson Beach, Tennessee Valley, Golden Gate National Recreation Area, Sausalito, Tiburon

attractions.name: Muir Beach & Muir Beach Overlook
attractions.description: Muir Beach is a quiet cove, beach and lagoon favored by locals, located on the Pacific Coast three miles west of the Golden Gate Bridge within the Golden Gate National Recreation Area. The beach features Redwood Creek Lagoon, a critical habitat for coho salmon and red-legged frogs. Adjacent Muir Beach Overlook provides expansive views of the Pacific Coast and Point Reyes Peninsula, making it a popular spot for whale watching and photography.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily, year-round
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Redwood Creek Lagoon and riparian habitat for coho salmon
Muir Beach Overlook with panoramic Pacific Coast views
Whale watching opportunities during migration seasons
Monarch butterfly wintering grounds in autumn
Clothing-optional sunbathing area at northern end of beach
Six beach fire rings (May-November) for campfires
attractions.tips: Always watch the waves — there are no lifeguards at Muir Beach. The overlook trail involves steep cliffs, so avoid it if you're afraid of heights. Cell phone coverage is unreliable, so rescue crews are farther away. The overlook is scenic but windy — bring layers. Dogs are allowed on leash in the beach area. Fires are only allowed in provided fire rings and must be extinguished with water only.
attractions.nearby: Stinson Beach, Slide Ranch (farm experience between Muir and Stinson beaches), Muir Woods National Monument, Golden Gate Bridge, Point Reyes National Seashore

attractions.name: Muir Woods National Monument
attractions.description: Muir Woods National Monument is an old-growth coast redwood forest located just north of San Francisco, federally protected since 1908. The monument features towering redwoods reaching hundreds of feet tall, with a network of trails and boardwalks winding through Redwood Creek. It offers a serene natural escape with its primeval forest ecosystem, serving as both refuge and living laboratory. The monument is managed by the National Park Service as part of the Golden Gate National Recreation Area.
attractions.hours: Open daily from 8:00 AM to sunset. Hours vary seasonally based on sunrise/sunset times.
attractions.admission: Standard Entrance Pass: $15.00 per adult (16 years and older); Children under 16: Free; Annual Entrance Pass: $45.00 (valid 12 months, admits pass holder and up to 3 adults); America the Beautiful Pass: FREE–$250.00 (covers entrance at all federal recreation sites). Parking/shuttle reservations are required and fees are separate from entrance fees.
highlights: Old-growth coast redwood forest with trees over 250 feet tall
Wooden boardwalk trails through dense redwood groves
Redwood Creek running through the monument
Part of Golden Gate National Recreation Area
Freed from logging since 1908 — one of the few remaining old-growth redwood stands near a major city
Network of hiking trails including the Main Loop Trail and extended trails into Mount Tamalpais
attractions.tips: Parking or shuttle reservations are required and must be made in advance at GoMuirWoods.com — do not drive there without a reservation. Visit early in the morning or on weekdays to avoid crowds. Children under 16 enter free. The monument is closed on Christmas Day. Wear layers as the forest is cooler and foggier than nearby areas. Cell service is limited or unavailable in the woods. Check the NPS alerts page before visiting for trail closures or weather conditions.
attractions.nearby: Mount Tamalpais State Park, Stinson Beach, Sausalito, Muir Beach, Golden Gate National Recreation Area, Tennessee Valley, Dipsea Trail

attractions.name: Point Bonita Lighthouse
attractions.description: Point Bonita Lighthouse is the third oldest lighthouse on the West Coast of the United States, welcoming mariners to the San Francisco Bay since 1855. Perched dramatically on a cliff edge at the entrance to the Golden Gate, it offers stunning views of the Pacific Ocean, Golden Gate Bridge, and Marin Headlands. Currently operated by the U.S. Coast Guard within Golden Gate National Recreation Area, the lighthouse is accessible via a scenic coastal trail and a historic suspension bridge.
attractions.address: Point Bonita Trailhead, Conzelman Road (near Battery Wallace)
attractions.hours: Seasonal hours vary. Extended summer schedule (through September): Saturday & Sunday 10:30 AM – 3:30 PM; Monday, Thursday & Friday 12:30 PM – 3:30 PM; Tuesday & Wednesday CLOSED. Off-season schedule (September–May): Typically Saturday, Sunday & Monday 12:30 PM – 3:30 PM; Tuesday–Friday CLOSED. Currently (as of April 2026), the Point Bonita Trail is open only on the third Saturday of each month per NPS. Last entry through the tunnel is at 3:20 PM sharp.
attractions.days_open: Varies by season: Summer (Sat, Sun, Mon, Thu, Fri); Off-season (Sat, Sun, Mon); Currently limited to third Saturday of each month
attractions.admission: Free admission. No entrance fees or parking fees. Guided Sunset Tours require advance reservation via recreation.gov.
highlights: Third oldest lighthouse on the West Coast of the United States (established 1855)
Iconic suspension bridge connecting visitors to the lighthouse
Spectacular views of the Pacific Ocean, Golden Gate Bridge, and San Francisco Bay
Scenic coastal trail through the Marin Headlands with harbor seal and marine wildlife viewing
Active U.S. Coast Guard lighthouse with historic laser foghorn
Ranger-led Light Chats and guided Sunset Tours available
attractions.tips: Check the NPS website or call ahead before visiting, as hours vary by season and the trail can close due to inclement weather or high winds (gusts above 30 mph). Arrive well before the 3:20 PM cutoff for last entry through the tunnel — visit early to avoid crowds. No pets, bikes, or smoking are permitted on the Point Bonita Trail. Parking is very limited at the trailhead; overflow parking is available at Battery Alexander. Bring layers — it can be windy and chilly even on sunny days. Binoculars are recommended for viewing marine life. The suspension bridge is currently closed until further notice, but the viewing platform remains accessible.
attractions.nearby: Battery Wallace, Battery Alexander, Marin Headlands, Golden Gate Bridge Vista Point, Fort Baker, Muir Beach, Rodeo Beach

attractions.name: Point Reyes National Seashore
attractions.description: Point Reyes National Seashore is a 71,028-acre marine reserve and protected area on the Point Reyes Peninsula in Marin County, California. From thunderous ocean breakers crashing against rocky headlands to expansive sand beaches, open grasslands, brushy hillsides, and forested ridges, it offers over 1,500 species of plants and animals. Home to several cultures over thousands of years, the Seashore preserves a tapestry of stories and interactions of people along the Pacific Coast.
attractions.hours: 6:00 AM - 12:00 AM (midnight)
attractions.days_open: Open every day of the year
attractions.admission: Free - No entrance pass required. No fees for parking at visitor centers, beaches, trailheads, or for day-use activities such as hiking, wildlife viewing, bicycle riding, kayaking, horse riding, fishing, attending ranger-led programs, visiting the Point Reyes Lighthouse, or picnicking (25 persons or fewer). Fees are charged for overnight camping and special use permits.
highlights: Point Reyes Lighthouse - one of the most iconic lighthouses on the California coast
Over 150 miles of hiking trails through diverse ecosystems including coastal bluffs, forested ridges, and open grasslands
Abundant wildlife viewing including tule elk, elephant seals, gray whales, and diverse bird species
Tule Elk Preserve - a protected herd of tule elk in their natural habitat
Drakes Estero and Tomales Point - scenic coastal wilderness areas
Bear Valley Visitor Center and Kenneth C. Patrick Visitor Center with interpretive exhibits
attractions.tips: The park is open 6 AM to midnight daily. Visitor centers have separate, limited hours so check ahead. The park is large and spread out - plan your route carefully. Dress in layers as fog and wind are common even on sunny days. Bring plenty of water as there are few services within the park. Cell phone coverage is limited or non-existent in many areas. Reservations are required for camping via Recreation.gov. The lighthouse stairs (over 300 steps) have seasonal closures for maintenance.
attractions.nearby: Muir Woods National Monument, Golden Gate National Recreation Area, Tomales Bay State Park, Marshall Store (oysters), Point Reyes Station (dining and shops), San Geronimo Valley

attractions.name: San Pablo Bay National Wildlife Refuge
attractions.description: San Pablo Bay National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974 to support migratory birds, wetland-dependent species, and the endangered salt marsh harvest mouse and California Ridgway's rail. The refuge features mudflats as far as the eye can see, vast stretches of lush pickleweed marsh, and a sense of solitude in the midst of seven million people. It protects critical tidal wetland habitats in the northern San Francisco Bay Area.
attractions.hours: Sunrise to sunset daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Critical habitat for the endangered salt marsh harvest mouse
Home to the California Ridgway's rail
7,000+ acres of tidal wetland and pickleweed marsh
Migratory bird sanctuary with vast mudflats
Free public access for hiking, birdwatching, and nature photography
Scenic trails through diverse marsh ecosystems
attractions.tips: The refuge is open daily from sunrise to sunset. The headquarters office is generally open Monday-Friday but staff are often in the field, so it's best to call 510-737-1252 before arriving. The refuge is free to visit, and it's excellent for birdwatching, hiking, and nature photography. Wear sturdy shoes for trail walking and bring binoculars for wildlife viewing.
attractions.nearby: Sonoma Raceway, San Francisco Bay Trail, Sears Point, Sonoma County Wine Country, Mare Island Naval Shipyard, Vallejo

attractions.name: Sausalito Waterfront (Bridgeway Promenade)
attractions.description: The Sausalito Waterfront, centered around the Bridgeway Promenade, is a picturesque waterfront district in Marin County offering stunning views of the San Francisco skyline and Golden Gate Bridge. The 0.4-mile paved promenade runs from Richardson Street to Princess Street, lined with boutiques, art galleries, restaurants, and cafes. Known for its European-inspired charm and relaxed coastal atmosphere, it is a popular destination for both locals and tourists.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (public waterfront area); individual shops and restaurants have varying hours, typically 10:00 AM - 9:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free (the waterfront promenade and public areas are free to access)
highlights: Stunning Golden Gate Bridge and San Francisco skyline views from the waterfront
Charming Bridgeway Promenade with boutique shops, art galleries, and waterfront dining
Unique houseboat communities along the Sausalito waterfront
Scenic ferry access from San Francisco's Pier 41
Dog-friendly Duffy boat rentals for sunset bay cruises
Battery Spencer nearby for iconic Golden Gate Bridge photography
attractions.tips: Take the ferry from San Francisco's Pier 41 for scenic bay views and to avoid parking hassles. Visit on weekday mornings for fewer crowds. Walk or bike across the Golden Gate Bridge for a memorable approach. Don't miss the houseboat communities and Battery Spencer for iconic views. Consider renting a Duffy boat for a unique sunset cruise on the bay.
attractions.nearby: Battery Spencer, Golden Gate Bridge, Marin Headlands, Bay Model Visitor Center, Houseboat Communities, Fort Baker, Muir Woods National Monument

attractions.name: Sonoma Coast State Park
attractions.description: Sonoma Coast State Park is a State of California property in Sonoma County consisting of public access use on lands adjoining the Pacific Ocean. The park spans approximately 17 miles of coastline from Bodega Head to Vista Trail (about 4 miles north of Jenner), featuring long sandy beaches below rugged headlands, craggy coastline with natural arches, secluded coves, and a marine terrace with coastal prairie habitat. The park is one of California's most scenic coastal attractions, offering opportunities for beachcombing, fishing, picnicking, and camping along Highway 1.
attractions.hours: Day Use: Dawn to 1 hour after sunset (generally sunrise to sunset). Bodega Dunes Day Use: 8:00 AM to sunset. Jenner Visitor Center: Weekdays 11:00 AM – 3:00 PM, Weekends 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM. Camping: Check-in times vary by campground.
attractions.days_open: Open daily, year-round (weather and conditions permitting)
attractions.admission: Day Use: $8.00 per vehicle; $7.00 for seniors (62+); Bus (10-24 passengers): $50.00; Bus (25+ passengers): $100.00. Many locations are free. Camping: Bodega Dunes $35.00/night, Hike & Bike $6.00/night; Wright's Beach $35.00/night, Premium $45.00/night; Extra vehicle: $8.00 ($7.00 for seniors).
highlights: 17 miles of stunning Pacific coastline with sandy beaches, rocky bluffs, and natural sea arch formations
Goat Rock Beach — home to a colony of harbor seals and iconic sea stack formations
Bodega Head — excellent whale watching spot with bluff-top views and hiking trails
Kortum Trail — a 3.8-mile coastal hiking trail connecting multiple beaches with ocean views
Bodega Dunes Campground — 99 campsites with hot showers, flush toilets, and beach access
Multiple tide pools and beaches including Shell Beach, Salmon Creek Beach, and Duncan's Landing
attractions.tips: The beach is NOT for swimming — strong rip currents, cold water, and sudden ground swells make surf play dangerous. Never turn your back to the ocean and keep children away from the water line. Bring layered clothing as coastal fog and wind are common even in summer. The park is not recommended for swimming; visit nearby Doran Regional Park in Bodega Bay for safer water access. Check weather and road conditions before visiting. Many day-use areas require packing out all trash. The Bodega Dunes accessible boardwalk is permanently closed due to storm damage.
attractions.nearby: Fort Ross State Historic Park, Armstrong Redwoods State Natural Reserve, Bodega Bay (whale watching and seafood), Doran Regional Park, Jenner Visitor Center, Point Reyes National Seashore, Russian River, Sonoma County wine country

attractions.name: Stinson Beach
attractions.description: Stinson Beach is an unincorporated community and census-designated place in Marin County, California, on the west coast of the United States. Located east-southeast of Bolinas, it sits on a 3.5-mile sandy beach with an elevation of 26 feet and a population of 541 at the 2020 census. It is a popular day trip destination for San Francisco Bay Area residents and Northern California tourists, known for being one of the cleanest and sandiest beaches in the state.
attractions.hours: Sunrise to sunset (beach hours may vary seasonally)
attractions.admission: Free (no entrance fee; parking at Stinson Beach parking lot is currently free, with fees implementation planned for 2024)
highlights: 3.5-mile sandy beach with gentle surf
Part of Golden Gate National Recreation Area
One of the cleanest beaches in California
Scenic Pacific Coast Highway (State Route 1) location
Hiking trails connecting to Mount Tamalpais
Popular day trip from San Francisco (about 35 minutes from Golden Gate Bridge)
attractions.tips: Visit on weekdays to avoid weekend crowds. The beach is one of the cleanest and sandiest in California, unlike neighboring Bolinas which is rockier. Parking is currently free but may have fees implemented in 2024. The beach is open sunrise to sunset. Bring layers as coastal fog and wind are common. Consider taking the daily bus service from Marin City if you prefer not to drive.
attractions.nearby: Muir Woods National Monument, Muir Beach, Bolinas Lagoon, Mount Tamalpais State Park, Duxbury Reef, Tennessee Valley

attractions.name: Tiburon Waterfront
attractions.description: Tiburon Waterfront refers to the scenic shoreline district of Tiburon, a charming incorporated town on the Tiburon Peninsula in Marin County, reaching south into San Francisco Bay. The area features Main Street waterfront dining, yacht harbors, ferry access to Angel Island and San Francisco, and stunning views of the Golden Gate Bridge and San Francisco skyline. Visitors can enjoy waterfront trails, parks like Shoreline Park and Paradise Beach Park, and historic restaurants such as Sam's Anchor Cafe, which has operated since 1920.
attractions.address: Main Street Waterfront District, Tiburon, CA 94920
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily (public waterfront area). Individual businesses, parks, and ferry services have their own hours.
attractions.admission: Free (public waterfront area). Parking fees may apply in town lots ($5 daily; $10 two-day; $15 three-day). Angel Island Ferry: $14 regular / $8 with Clipper Card (one-way).
highlights: Historic Main Street waterfront dining with restaurants like Sam's Anchor Cafe (est. 1920)
Angel Island Ferry terminal with service to San Francisco Ferry Building
Scenic waterfront trails including the Old Rail Trail for walking and biking
Stunning views of the Golden Gate Bridge, Alcatraz, and San Francisco skyline
Sailing and marina access with yacht harbors along Richardson Bay
Shoreline Park and Paradise Beach Park for picnics and recreation
attractions.tips: Parking on weekends and holidays can be challenging — arrive early or use the Tiburon-Angel Island Ferry from San Francisco. Street parking is limited to 2 hours on weekdays. For the best waterfront views, visit on a clear day and walk the Old Rail Trail from the ferry landing to Paradise Beach Park. Sam's Anchor Cafe is a must-visit landmark — try to get a seat on their rear deck for bay views.
attractions.nearby: Angel Island State Park, Blackie's Pasture, Belvedere Island, Sausalito waterfront, Corte Madera Creek Ecological Reserve, Mount Tamalpais State Park

attractions.name: Almaden Quicksilver County Park
attractions.description: Almaden Quicksilver County Park is a 4,163-acre park that includes the grounds of former mercury ("quicksilver") mines adjacent to south San Jose, California. The park features steep hillsides, cool forests, open meadows, deep valleys, and borders the Guadalupe and Almaden Reservoirs. It is owned by the County of Santa Clara and managed by the Santa Clara County Parks and Recreation Department.
attractions.hours: 8:00 AM to Sunset
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 4,163 acres of diverse terrain including forests, meadows, and valleys
Historic New Almaden Quicksilver Mines — produced over 83 million pounds of mercury
Senador Mine ruins and other mining structure remains
Sweeping views over the Santa Clara Valley and San Jose
Multiple trail entrances: Hacienda, Mockingbird Hill, McAbee, and Hicks/Wood Road
Borders Guadalupe and Almaden Reservoirs
attractions.tips: Dogs must be kept on leash. The park is open year-round from 8 a.m. to sunset and is free to visit. Multiple entrances with parking lots are available — Hacienda Entrance is the primary access point. Many facilities are disability accessible. The nearby Casa Grande Almaden Quicksilver Mining Museum is worth visiting for historical context.
attractions.nearby: Casa Grande Almaden Quicksilver Mining Museum, Almaden Lake Regional Park, Guadalupe Reservoir, Mount Umunhum, Almaden Reservoir

attractions.name: Computer History Museum
attractions.description: The Computer History Museum in Mountain View, California, is the world's leading institution dedicated to preserving and presenting the stories and artifacts of the Information Age. It houses an extensive collection of computing artifacts, from early mechanical calculators to modern smartphones and AI systems. Visitors can explore interactive exhibits, live demos including the famous IBM 1401 Demo Lab, and rare Apple prototypes.
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Sunday: 10 a.m.–5 p.m. (Closed Monday and Tuesday)
attractions.admission: Adult (Ages 18+): $21.50 | Senior (Ages 65+): $16.50 | Student (Ages 11+, including university students): $16.50 | Military (Active military with ID): $16.50 | Educator (With ID): $16.50 | Youth (Ages 8–10): $6.00 | Child (Ages 0–7): Free
highlights: IBM 1401 Demo Lab with live demonstrations of the vintage mainframe computer
Rare Apple prototypes including the Apple I, iPod, and iPhone
Revolutionaries exhibition featuring pioneers of the computing industry
Interactive exhibits including Pong gaming, self-driving car experience, and coding activities
World's largest collection of computing artifacts spanning 2,000 years of history
Bistro Café and Museum Store on-site
attractions.tips: Plan to spend 2-3 hours to fully explore the museum. The IBM 1401 Demo Lab runs scheduled demonstrations — check the daily schedule upon arrival. Free parking is available in the museum lot. General admission includes access to all exhibits, demos, the Bistro Café, and Museum store. Members get free admission. The museum is closed on Mondays and Tuesdays.
attractions.nearby: Googleplex (Google headquarters), NASA Ames Research Center, Moffett Field Historical Society Museum, Shoreline Park, Rengstorff House, Computer History Museum is located in the heart of Silicon Valley near many tech company campuses.

attractions.name: Don Edwards San Francisco Bay National Wildlife Refuge
attractions.description: The nation's first urban national wildlife refuge, established in 1972 through local activism, sits on the southern end of San Francisco Bay. This wildlife oasis in an urban sea encompasses 30,000 acres of habitat for millions of migratory birds and endangered species. With 15 different habitat types, the refuge provides critical habitat for threatened and endangered species while offering opportunities for people to enjoy nature through trails, birding, and environmental education.
attractions.hours: Vehicle entrance gate: 7:00 am - 7:00 pm; Fremont Headquarters/Visitor Center: 7:00 am - 5:00 pm; Alviso Environmental Education Center: hours vary; Trails: sunrise to sunset
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: First urban national wildlife refuge in the United States
30,000 acres of diverse wetland and marsh habitat
Home to 15 different habitat types and millions of migratory birds
Multiple visitor locations including Fremont HQ, Alviso Environmental Education Center, Bair Island, and Ravenswood
Extensive trail system open sunrise to sunset for hiking and birding
Environmental education programs at the Alviso center
attractions.tips: Visit during sunrise or sunset for the best birding and photography opportunities. The refuge is open daily but hours vary by location, so check specific site hours before visiting. Bring binoculars for wildlife viewing. Wear layers as bay weather can change quickly. Multiple locations are spread across the South Bay, so plan which sites you want to visit.
attractions.nearby: Alviso Marina County Park, Newhall Slough, Coyote Creek Trail, San Jose Guadalupe River Park, Coyote Point Recreation Area

attractions.name: Hakone Estate and Gardens
attractions.description: Hakone Estate and Gardens is one of the oldest residential-style Japanese gardens open to the public in the Western Hemisphere, established as a private summer retreat in 1915. The 18-acre property features traditional Japanese landscaping including hill and pond gardens, a Zen garden, a tea garden, and a bamboo garden, along with historic buildings authentically crafted in Japan over 100 years ago. It is a recipient of the Save America's Treasures Award by the National Trust for Historic Preservation and serves as a cultural venue hosting events, art exhibits, and tea ceremonies.
attractions.hours: Summer Hours (March 9 - October 31): Monday-Friday 10:00 am - 5:00 pm (last admission 4:30 pm), Saturday-Sunday 11:00 am - 5:00 pm (last admission 4:00 pm). Winter Hours (November 1 - March 15): Monday-Friday 10:00 am - 4:00 pm (last admission 3:30 pm), Saturday-Sunday 11:00 am - 4:00 pm (last admission 3:00 pm).
attractions.days_open: Open daily, closed on Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year's Day
attractions.admission: Adult (18-64): $12, Seniors (65+): $10, Youth (5-17): $8, Children (4 and under): Free. Saratoga residents visit free on the 1st Tuesday of each month with ID verification.
highlights: Hill and Pond Garden with koi ponds and waterfalls (oldest garden, completed 1918)
Zen Garden
Tea Garden with authentic tea houses
Bamboo Garden (completed 1987)
Cultural Exchange Center - authentic reproduction of a 19th-century Kyoto tea merchant's house
Annual Cherry Blossom Festival
attractions.tips: Visit during cherry blossom season (late March to mid-April) for spectacular blooms, but expect larger crowds. Saratoga residents can visit free on the first Tuesday of each month with ID. Last admission is 30 minutes before closing on weekdays and 1 hour before on weekends. Download the garden map from the official website before your visit.
attractions.nearby: Villa Montalvo (Saratoga), Mountain Winery, Saratoga Village, Castle Rock State Park, Stevens Creek County Park

attractions.name: Happy Hollow Park & Zoo
attractions.description: Happy Hollow Park & Zoo is a 16-acre zoo and amusement park in San Jose, California, originally opened in 1961. It features an AZA-accredited zoo with over 140 animals, family rides, a puppet theater, playgrounds, and a carousel. The park closed for major renovations in 2008 and reopened in 2010.
attractions.hours: Open days: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM. Closed days: Monday, Tuesday. (Hours may vary seasonally; check website for current schedule.)
attractions.admission: Children under age 2: FREE; General Admission (Ages 2-59): $22; Senior Admission (Ages 60+): $19; Military Admission: $19; Reciprocal Member Admission: $11; Membership Guest: $11; Groups of 14+ (Feb-Aug rate): $14; Daily Parking: $10; Annual Parking Pass: $80
highlights: AZA-accredited zoo with over 140 animals including lemurs, red pandas, jaguars, and crocodiles
Amusement rides for toddlers and children including a carousel and roller coaster
Puppet theater with live performances
Petting zoo with an animal barn featuring miniature horses, sheep, goats, and cows
Nature play areas with maze, slides, swings, ropes, and climbing structures
Education center offering year-round classes for ages 12 months to adult
attractions.tips: Arrive early on weekends to avoid crowds and long ride lines; the park gets busiest late morning. Bring quarters for the petting zoo food pellets (25 cents). Parking is $10 daily — consider the annual parking pass if visiting multiple times. AAA members receive discounted admission. Admissions and membership sales end 30 minutes before closing. Plan for at least 2-3 hours for a full visit.
attractions.nearby: The Tech Interactive (1.6 mi), History Park at Kelley Park (adjacent), San Jose City Hall and Plaza (1.7 mi), San Jose Museum of Art (2.1 mi), SAP Center (2.3 mi)

attractions.name: History Park at Kelley Park
attractions.description: History Park at Kelley Park is a 14-acre indoor/outdoor living history museum in San Jose, California, featuring 32 original and replica buildings arranged to resemble a small American town from the early 1900s. Since its opening in 1971, the park has preserved historic homes, businesses, and landmarks from the Santa Clara Valley, including a working print shop, antique trolleys, and an old-fashioned ice cream parlor. The park is operated by History San José and also houses the Viet Museum and Portuguese Historical Museum.
attractions.hours: Daily 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM (Park grounds); Historic Houses and Museums open Saturday & Sunday 12:00 PM – 3:45 PM; History Park Trolley runs Saturday & Sunday 12:00 PM – 3:15 PM; Gift Shop open Saturday & Sunday 11:30 AM – 3:45 PM; O'Brien's Ice Cream Parlor open Saturday & Sunday 11:00 AM – 3:30 PM; Park Tours Wednesdays & Fridays at 1:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free admission to the park grounds. Special events may have admission fees. Parking fee applies.
highlights: 32 historic and replica buildings representing early 1900s Santa Clara Valley life
Operating antique trolleys (horse-drawn and electric) on weekends
Pacific Hotel with O'Brien's Ice Cream Parlor and Candy Shop
Half-scale replica of the 1881 San Jose Electric Light Tower
Viet Museum and Portuguese Historical Museum housed within the park
Working print shop and guided park tours on Wednesdays and Fridays
attractions.tips: Visit on weekends to see the historic buildings and museums open (they have limited hours during the week). The trolley rides are free and run on Saturdays and Sundays. Park tours are available Wednesdays and Fridays at 1 PM during the school year. Check the website for special events as they may have separate admission fees. Parking is available off Phelan Avenue. The park is closed on major holidays including New Year's Day, Easter, Independence Day, Memorial Day, Labor Day, and Christmas.
attractions.nearby: Happy Hollow Park & Zoo, Japanese Friendship Garden, Winchester Mystery House, Children's Discovery Museum of San Jose, San Jose Museum of Art, Tech Interactive

attractions.name: Palo Alto Baylands Nature Preserve
attractions.description: The Palo Alto Baylands Nature Preserve is the largest tract of undisturbed marshland remaining in the San Francisco Bay. The 1,940-acre preserve encompasses both Palo Alto and East Palo Alto and features fifteen miles of multi-use trails through a unique mixture of tidal and fresh water habitats. It is an important habitat for migratory shorebirds and is considered one of the best birdwatching spots on the West Coast. The preserve includes the Lucy Evans Baylands Nature Interpretive Center, a sailing station, duck pond, and picnic areas.
attractions.hours: 7:00 AM – 8:30 PM daily (hours may vary seasonally; trails generally open dawn to dusk)
attractions.days_open: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
attractions.admission: Free
attractions.phone: (650) 329-2506 (Lucy Evans Baylands Nature Interpretive Center), (650) 617-3156 (Ranger Station)
highlights: 1,940 acres of undisturbed marshland — the largest remaining in the San Francisco Bay
15 miles of multi-use trails for hiking, biking, and birdwatching
Lucy Evans Baylands Nature Interpretive Center with educational programs and exhibits
Important habitat for migratory shorebirds, endangered Ridgway's Rail, and salt marsh harvest mouse
Dog-friendly trails (dogs must be on leash)
Duck pond, sailing station, and public picnic areas
attractions.tips: Hours vary seasonally — call ahead or check signage for current closing times. Dress in layers as the Baylands can be windy and temperatures vary significantly. Parking is available in several small lots along Embarcadero Road. Bring binoculars for the best birdwatching experience. Stay on designated trails to protect sensitive habitats. The preserve is dog-friendly but dogs must be kept on leash. Visit the Interpretive Center first for maps and trail information.
attractions.nearby: Palo Alto Municipal Golf Course, Palo Alto Airport, Stanford University, Stanford Shopping Center, Shoreline Park (Mountain View), Googleplex, California Academy of Sciences (San Francisco), San Francisco Bay waterfront

attractions.name: Rosicrucian Egyptian Museum
attractions.description: The Rosicrucian Egyptian Museum is an educational institution devoted to ancient Egypt, located in the Rose Garden neighborhood of San Jose, California. It houses the largest collection of ancient Egyptian artifacts on exhibit in western North America, featuring objects from pre-dynastic times through Egypt's early Islamic era. The museum is architecturally inspired by the Temple of Amon at Karnak and includes a replica rock-cut tomb, a Peace Garden, and an AI-driven Thoth animatronic exhibit.
attractions.hours: Friday: 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM; Saturday and Sunday: 11:00 AM - 6:00 PM
attractions.admission: Adults (18-64): $15.00; Adults (65+): $12.00; Students with ID: $12.00; Children (ages 7-17): $10.00; Children under 7: Free; Rosicrucian Members and Friends of the Museum: Free. No cash accepted; credit/debit cards only.
highlights: Largest collection of ancient Egyptian artifacts on exhibit in western North America (over 4,000 artifacts)
Life-size replica of a rock-cut tomb that visitors can walk through
Peace Garden with authentic 18th Dynasty Egyptian landscaping and a labyrinth
Thoth AI animatronic — an interactive robot that answers questions about ancient Egypt
Karnak-style entrance architecture inspired by the Temple of Amon at Karnak
Self-guided audio tour available via museum app
attractions.tips: Purchase tickets online in advance to speed entry; the museum sells out on weekends. Download the museum app before arriving for the self-guided audio tour. Photography without flash is permitted in galleries. The museum is only open Friday through Sunday, so plan accordingly. Allocate 2-3 hours for your visit. There is no souvenir shop on-site. Parking is available on street and in nearby lots.
attractions.nearby: Municipal Rose Garden (0.3 mi), J. Lohr Vineyards & Wines (0.6 mi), Winchester Mystery House (1.9 mi), Santana Row (1.6 mi), The Tech Interactive (1.8 mi), Children's Discovery Museum (1.8 mi), Cathedral Basilica of St. Joseph (1.8 mi), San Pedro Square Market (1.6 mi), SAP Center (1.2 mi)

attractions.name: San José Museum of Art
attractions.description: The San José Museum of Art is a leading contemporary art museum in downtown San José, dedicated to nurturing empathy and connection through socially relevant contemporary art.
